retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis Screening -- Interfering Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Interreader variability should be highlighted as a significant cause of false-positive and false-negative results. Results have been shown to vary up to 15% between different personnel interpreting the test. [8] This figure increases when those with little prior experience read the test. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Interferon Test -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: Interpretation of TB blood test results depends on the specific IGRA used. For the QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus, results are based on the amount of IFN-γ released in response to M tuberculosis antigens and control substances after incubation. In contrast, the T-SPOT.TB assay is interpreted by counting the IFN-γ–producing cells (eg, spots) after the blood is incubated with the antigens. Laboratories typically report both qualitative and quantitative results. The qualitative results for QFT-Plus are categorized as positive, negative, or indeterminate, whereas T-SPOT.TB results may be positive, borderline, negative, or invalid. Quantitative results include numerical values indicating responses to the TB antigens and 2 controls: nil (negative control) and mitogen (positive control). Although no standardized guidelines exist for interpreting the quantitative values alone, the numerical values can help interpret borderline or conflicting results, particularly when combined with clinical risk factors. [16] [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis Screening -- Normal and Critical Findings -- Interpreting the TST
retrieved_docs.content: In patients vaccinated with BCG and have a positive PPD reaction, the positive result is more likely due to tuberculosis exposure than the remote vaccination, and the immunization history should be disregarded when considering whether to offer TB-preventative therapy. [13] IGRA tests are advantageous in this situation, as these are unaffected by prior BCG vaccination. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis Screening -- Normal and Critical Findings -- Interpreting the TST
retrieved_docs.content: In the latter group, consisting primarily of older adults, a second TST ("booster") applied a couple of weeks later often will be positive. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis Screening -- Normal and Critical Findings -- Interpreting the TST
retrieved_docs.content: If the TST is negative, experts' opinion outlines that repeat testing with another TST or an IGRA is reasonable if the patient is at high risk of infection progression or recent infection for further evaluation for the possibility of a false negative test. [13] However, not all of these cases would be amenable to repeat testing, and decisions to retest should be made on a case-by-case basis. Attention should be drawn to situations in which repeat testing may be considered due to the increased risk of a false negative test:

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis Screening -- Indications -- Test Selection
retrieved_docs.content: The TST is the test of choice for children younger than 5 or when there is a concern for a waning immune response, as the TST has a boosting effect. [13] The TST may be somewhat preferred for patients, such as healthcare workers, who need repeat testing annually because IGRA test results may be less reproducible. [13] Issues with the reproducibility of IGRAs stem from several factors, including specimen handling, manufacturing processes, and immunological considerations, such as prior boosting with a TST. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis Screening -- Normal and Critical Findings -- Interpreting the TST
retrieved_docs.content: Alternatively, a false positive test should be suspected in low-risk patients for prior exposure or disease progression. [13] For this reason, these patients should not be tested unless otherwise mandated. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis Screening -- Indications -- Test Selection
retrieved_docs.content: Simultaneous or sequential testing is not recommended, except in cases where the initial test is negative, indeterminate, or borderline if the clinical suspicion is high. In that case, repeat testing with TST or IGRA (depending on which test was done initially) can be done. Additionally, the TST or IGRA should not be repeated if they were previously positive or to follow therapy, as this positive result is expected to be retained, and differences in results are challenging to interpret. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis Screening -- Interfering Factors
retrieved_docs.content: The varying causes of the TST's false positive and false negative results are outlined in the table below. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Interferon Test -- Interfering Factors
retrieved_docs.content: When the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention recommends TST to diagnose latent tuberculosis, IGRAs may be used instead or as an adjunct in concordance with the established criteria. Several sources of variability can affect IGRA results, some of which are not yet fully understood. Variation in reliability can include statistical variability in obtaining analytical data, responsive variability dependent on the immune response, and manufacturing variability.

retrieved_docs.title: PPD Skin Test -- Interfering Factors -- False-positive Reaction
retrieved_docs.content: Falsely positive test results occur when the test result is positive in the absence of M. tuberculosis infection. Since the PPD test has low specificity, low-risk individuals with a positive test may be false positives. A false-positive reaction may be seen in the following cases: [2] Previous vaccination with Bacillus Calmette-Guerin (BCG) Infections with non-tuberculous mycobacteria Improper administration of the test Incorrect reading or misinterpretation of the test The IGRA test may be considered in individuals with prior BCG vaccination since childhood BCG immunization will not affect IGRA results.

retrieved_docs.title: Genitourinary Tuberculosis -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Tuberculin skin testing: This common skin test uses a standardized solution made with a purified protein derivative (PPD) where a measured amount is injected under the skin. [144] In patients with an Mtb infection, this will produce a T-cell-mediated delayed-type hypersensitivity skin reaction. [144] The test requires 2 patient visits and will be positive in patients with previous BCG infection or treatment. [143] [144] See the companion StatPearls' article on the " PPD Skin Test " for more information and details. [144]

retrieved_docs.title: Interferon Test -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic tests for latent TB have played a role in the decreasing prevalence of the disease over time. In addition, the development of vaccines for the prevention of TB in endemic areas and effective treatments for active TB have also contributed to a reduction in the incidence. The IGRA test has several advantages over other testing methods for diagnosing latent TB, including a single patient encounter for sample collection, the possibility of obtaining results within 24 hours, its usefulness in patients with prior BCG vaccination, and results that are not subject to reader bias. However, if the patient is immunosuppressed, they may have an inadequate immune response, resulting in a false negative. IGRA tests are not appropriate for children younger than 5 for the same reason. Additionally, IGRA tests are not useful for monitoring treatment response. [2] [23] [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis Screening -- Normal and Critical Findings -- Interpreting IGRA
retrieved_docs.content: Results are reported as indeterminate, positive, or negative. An indeterminate test signifies a weak response of TH1 cells to the positive control (low mitogen) or a strong response to the negative control (high nil). [33] Both values are reported so that it is known to which situation an indeterminate test can be attributed. [34] Indeterminate tests due to a strong response to the negative control occur in cases of high background immune activity with high initial levels of IFN-γ, such as in rheumatologic states, or weak response to the positive control, such as immunosuppression. [35] Indeterminate results are due to low mitogen response an overwhelming majority of the time. [35] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Interferon Test -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: A positive IGRA test result generally indicates TB infection, but further evaluation, including chest radiography and clinical correlation, is needed to exclude active TB disease. A negative test result suggests that TB infection is unlikely. However, individuals with symptoms of TB infection or those at high risk (eg, immunocompromised patients) may have an active infection and a negative test result. An indeterminate (QFT-Plus), borderline, or invalid (T-SPOT) result indicates that the test is inconclusive. Repeating the IGRA or performing a TST may be helpful. [21] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis Overview -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The sensitivity and specificity of diagnostic tests can vary significantly depending on factors that include pretest probability, immunological status and age of the patient, timing of the test, adherence to proper test procedures, adequacy of specimen collection, and the ability to interpret test results in the context of the patient's risk factors and immune function. See Table. Tuberculosis Tests and Their Advantages and Disadvantages. Radiological images may demonstrate normal to markedly abnormal, nonspecific findings. Without a confirmatory culture or molecular assay, TB is presumptive and based on the degree of clinical suspicion concurrent with surrogate markers of infection. Both under and over-diagnosis occur, depending on the epidemiological setting. [17] [32] [33] [34] [35] [36]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1448
retrieved_docs.content: Pediatric Infectious Disease Terminology incorporates فحص التوبركولين (the tuberculin test) as a specific concept within its scope. فحص التوبركولين is classified as a type of immunizations and screening for infectious disease, highlighting its function in the detection of latent tuberculosis infection. Immunizations and screening for infectious disease serves as a broader category that also encompasses Other and nonspecific abnormal cytological, histological, immunological and DNA test findings, which are organized under this same parent heading. Collectively, these relationships demonstrate how the tuberculin test and various nonspecific laboratory abnormalities are situated within the overarching framework of infectious disease screening and immunization practices, with the pediatric terminology providing a focused context for the tuberculin test.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2573
retrieved_docs.content: Immunizations and screening for infectious disease is a broad clinical category that encompasses specific procedures such as فحص التوبركولين (the tuberculin test). Within this overarching category, there is a subcategory titled Other and nonspecific abnormal cytological, histological, immunological and DNA test findings, which groups together various atypical laboratory results that do not fit into more specific diagnostic groups. This subcategory further includes the specific assay تحليل الكروموسوم غير طبيعي (abnormal chromosome analysis), illustrating a hierarchical relationship in which general infectious‑disease screening contains a broader set of nonspecific abnormal test findings, and that set contains particular genetic‑chromosome analyses.

retrieved_docs.content: an ancillary indicator of tuberculin reactivity and may aid in assessing tuberculosis exposure risk. Collectively, these relationships highlight the interplay between diabetes mellitus, its genetic underpinnings, metabolic and vascular complications, medication contraindications, limb‑loss sequelae, immunologic testing outcomes, and ancillary markers such as cranial size in comprehensive patient evaluation and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1010
retrieved_docs.content: The positive tuberculin test (فحص التوبركولين إيجابي) is clinically associated with both appendectomy (استئصال الزائدة) and head circumference (حج القحف), indicating that these clinical parameters may be linked to tuberculin reactivity. In the broader context of patient evaluation, the tuberculin test is mapped to the category of immunizations and screening for infectious disease (immunizations and screening for infectious disease), reflecting its role as a standard screening tool for latent Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection. Within this screening framework, there exists a higher‑level group labeled “Other and nonspecific abnormal cytological, histological, immunological and DNA test findings,” which is considered a parent category of the infectious‑disease screening activities. A specific child of this group is abnormal chromosome analysis (تحليل الكروموسوم غير طبيعي), representing a particular type of nonspecific abnormal laboratory finding. Together, these relationships illustrate how a positive tuberculin test connects to surgical history, growth measurements, and broader infectious‑disease screening processes, while also being situated within a hierarchy of miscellaneous abnormal laboratory results that include chromosomal analyses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1842
retrieved_docs.content: Other and nonspecific abnormal cytological, histological, immunological and DNA test findings are categorized under the broader domain of immunizations and screening for infectious disease, and they encompass specific abnormalities such as تحليل الكروموسوم غير طبيعي (abnormal chromosome analysis). Within the immunizations and screening for infectious disease framework, a positive tuberculin test (فحص التوبركولين إيجابي) is a mapped indicator, and this positive tuberculin test is also clinically associated with head circumference (حج القحف). Consequently, the spectrum of nonspecific abnormal laboratory findings, including chromosome analysis, is linked to infectious‑disease screening practices, and a positive tuberculin result may have relevance to measurements of head circumference in clinical assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2518
retrieved_docs.content: Immunizations and screening for infectious disease are linked to فحص التوبركولين إيجابي, indicating that tuberculin testing is a component of infectious‑disease preventive care. In addition, حج القحف is clinically associated with فحص التوبركولين إيجابي, suggesting that measurements of head circumference may be relevant when evaluating a positive tuberculin test, particularly in contexts such as pediatric tuberculosis where cranial growth patterns can reflect disease involvement. Together, these relationships highlight the role of tuberculin screening within broader immunization programs and the potential clinical significance of head‑circumference assessment in patients with a positive tuberculin result.

retrieved_docs.content: Acute bronchitis is classified under the broader category الْتِهابِ شعبي. Clinically, acute bronchitis is associated with الْتِهاب رِئَوِيّ. The condition الْتِهاب رِئَوِيّ includes a subcategory called التهاب رئوي وليدي. Both الْتِهاب رِئَوِيّ and the measurement حج القحف are linked to a positive فحص التوبركولين, indicating that pulmonary inflammation and head circumference may be related to tuberculin reactivity. retrieved_docs.content: syndrome is recorded as a comorbid condition, underscoring the relevance of metabolic disturbances alongside perinatal and infectious disorders. Birth trauma is linked to intracerebral bleeding in the fetus or newborn, emphasizing the need for neurological assessment after traumatic deliveries. Head circumference measurement (حج القحف) is clinically associated with a positive tuberculin test, indicating that abnormal head size may raise suspicion for underlying mycobacterial infection or related immune response. Collectively, these multilingual classification hierarchies integrate perinatal growth restriction, respiratory and cardiovascular complications, neonatal infections, pharmacologic influences on early pregnancy loss, diabetes‑related comorbidities, and genetic mechanisms, thereby supporting comprehensive diagnosis, coding, and patient management across perinatal and neonatal care.
